title: refund — field classification
description: Refund data is a mix of operational identifiers (refund_id, refund_number), financial records (amount_cents, currency, provider_fee_reversal_cents — 7y financial retention), workflow-state metadata (status, approved_by, rejected_by), and marketing-adjacent metadata (issuance context tagging linked marketing conversion event). No health / medical / minor-specific data on any row.

public: Refund.refund_number (customer-facing — printed on refund confirmations, credit notes)
Refund.status (derived from state machine — safe to expose)
Refund.reason_note (customer-facing part; admin-only reasons like fraud_confirmed / accounting_correction render with a generic 'admin correction' label to non-privileged readers)
Refund.provider (public — helps customer support explain 'refund went via Stripe, contact them for details' scenarios)
confidential: Refund.amount_cents (customer-facing when viewing own refund; confidential across-tenant + across-customer)
Refund.provider_fee_reversal_cents (financial-competitive — reveals per-provider fee reversal terms)
restricted: Refund.reason_note (admin-only portion — when reason IN ('fraud_confirmed', 'admin_correction', 'accounting_correction'), the reason_note may contain internal investigation notes not appropriate for customer view)
Refund.metadata.bank_transfer_details (when refunded_to='bank_transfer' — encrypted at rest via TokenEncryptor from payment module; only decrypt for finance role during dispatch)
Refund.metadata.check_details (when refunded_to='check' — mailing address + tracking number)
regulated_financial: Refund.amount_cents + currency + provider_fee_reversal_cents (financial-audit alignment — 7 year retention)
Refund.credit_note_id (links to financial reversal document — 7y retention)
Refund.transaction_id (links to double-entry ledger movement — 7y retention)
Refund.status (state machine record — every transition audit-logged 7y)
Refund.approved_by_user_id + approved_at + rejected_by_user_id + rejected_at (segregation-of-duties audit — SOX §404)

notes: Refund.refund_number is deliberately public — the whole point is customer-facing display (printed on receipts + credit notes). The confidential fields are the *amounts* of the refund (amount_cents / currency / provider_fee_reversal_cents) — competitors gaining access to a tenant's refund ledger would learn per-provider fee reversal terms + refund-worthy revenue patterns.

Refund.reason_note is DOUBLE-CLASSIFIED — the customer-facing portion is `public` (customer sees why their refund succeeded/failed), the admin-only portion is `restricted` (internal investigation notes). Egress rendering routes through ReasonNoteRedactor which selects the customer-visible portion based on reader permissions.
No PII on the Refund row directly. Payment linkage flows through payment_id — the customer_id is on the parent payment, redacted via the payment module's PII gate.
regulated_financial fields align with 7-year retention. Extended to 10y with Enterprise `refund_extended_retention`.
No PCI-DSS scope: NO card data / PAN / CVV on any owned row. Refund uses payment_id -> payment.provider_reference_id (which is itself a token, never PAN).
provider_metadata is CONFIDENTIAL because it may reveal provider-side internals + business relationships. Only finance/admin/owner + support reads it (via refunds.view-financials permission).
